data.title: Cellnex Telecom Closing Valuation Gap With Peers Provides Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Cellnex Telecom (OTCPK:CLNXF) (OTCPK:CLLNY) is the largest owner and manager of cell phone towers in Europe, with in excess of 100,000 towers in its asset base. As the company doesn’t have the same brand/name recognition as its peers American Tower (AMT) and Crown Castle (CCI), and as it doesn’t have the status of a REIT, it's trading at lower multiples.

I think that valuation gap could be closed in the next few years as Cellnex is kicking off a more shareholder friendly policy. Rather than continuously investing in growing the business, the company now also plans rather aggressive buybacks and has pledged to pay a minimum dividend of 500M EUR per year going forward. Although the initial dividend yield will be relatively low at 2%, I expect this will attract a new type of investor.

Cellnex has its primary listing in Spain, where it's trading with CLNX as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Spain is 1.4 million shares, representing a monetary value of 45M EUR. The CLLNY listing is the most liquid listing in North America with an average daily volume of 87,000 shares, representing almost $2M per day. Keep in mind - one CLLNY share represents half of the underlying share of Cellnex Telecom. The current market cap is approximately 22.6B EUR. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

A look at the Q1 results confirms the strong underlying cash flows

While the company does not publish a fully detailed financial report, it does provide shareholders with enough information on a quarterly basis to stay well-informed.

In the first quarter of this year, the company reported total revenue of 964M EUR, which is a 2% increase compared to the same quarter a year ago. Meanwhile, the operating expenses remained unchanged (they actually decreased by 0.5%), resulting in a 20M EUR increase in the adjusted EBITDA, and this represents an increase of just more than 2.5%.

As Cellnex is a lease-heavy company, it also makes sense to have a look at the EBITDA after lease payments (‘EBITDAaL’), which increased by in excess of 5% to 566M EUR.

And while the income statement shows a net loss of 49M EUR, which is higher than the net loss recorded in Q1 2024, that loss is not very relevant to the investment thesis as the non-recurring expenses increased by 90M EUR (the company would otherwise have been able to report a net profit). Additionally, it’s also very encouraging to see the net financial expenses decreased from 234M EUR to 196M EUR (although the cash interest expenses increased, as I will show later).

When an investor looks at Cellnex, it shouldn’t really focus on the net income. Although it looks like a REIT, Cellnex isn’t a REIT and is operating as a normal corporation. This means it needs to apply a depreciation schedule to the value of its cell phone towers, whereas its competitors/peers with a REIT status don’t have to do so.

That’s why it is more important to have a look at the recurring levered free cash flow result. This takes the maintenance capex into account, and that maintenance capex is of course substantially lower than the depreciation and amortization expenses.

As the image above shows, the starting point is the 798M EUR adjusted EBITDA, resulting in a 551M EUR recurring operating free cash flow of 551M EUR after taking the 15M EUR in maintenance capex into account. But what really matters is the recurring levered FCF as the interest expenses are obviously a large part of the cost basis and while the net cash interest expenses increased to 151M EUR.

All these elements combined resulted in an 8.6% decrease of the recurring levered FCF, but there's an important element here. As the image below shows, the first quarter is disproportionally heavy when it comes to cash lease payments and cash interest payments. In fact, the total interest payment in Q1 is higher than the payments in Q2 and Q3 combined.

This means we certainly cannot multiply the Q1 levered free cash flow result by 4 to calculate the anticipated full-year result. And that’s why the company’s guidance for this year comes in handy. As you can see below, Cellnex is guiding for a RLFCF of 1.9-1.95B EUR and a net free cash flow (after growth investments) of 280-380M EUR.

As the company’s asset base is in constant evolution, its updated pro forma guidance for 2025 (adjusted for changes in its Irish and Austrian asset base) calls for a RLFCF of 1.92-1.97B EUR, but the longer-term target to generate 2-2.2B EUR in RLFCF in 2027 remains unchanged.

The company’s new dividend policy may attract a new type of investor

Until the capital markets day of last year, the company was focusing on growth, and the vast majority of its free cash flow was spent on growth initiatives. While these growth investments still are a major cash drain, Cellnex is taking substantial steps to becoming more shareholder friendly.

It will pay another rather symbolic) dividend of 1.67 cents per share this year (as a final dividend for the FY 2024 results), but the company plans to buy back a substantial number of shares before embarking on a more generous dividend program. Cellnex plans to repurchase 800M EUR of its own shares. And assuming an average share price of 35 EUR, this would allow the company to repurchase and cancel about 23M shares, or just over 3% of its share count. That would reduce the net share count from the current 705M shares to around 685M shares (I’m also taking some dilution into account from stock-based compensation).

This buyback will mean two things. First of all, the Recurring Levered Free Cash flow per share will increase. Using 1.9B EUR for this year, using a 3% lower share count will increase this from 2.7 EUR per share to almost 2.8 EUR per share. That’s a good evolution.

Secondly, as the company is promising its shareholders a targeted minimum dividend of 500M EUR per year, a lower share count obviously also boosts the dividend per share. The minimum dividend payment has been established at 500M EUR per year and divided over 685M shares, this represents 73 cents per share for a yield of just over 2%.

While that dividend yield isn’t shocking, it’s important to keep in mind this is the "minimum" dividend and Cellnex may decide to make higher dividend payments. But given the anticipated strong cash flows (first on an underlying basis and subsequently also on a reported basis from 2027 on), it may make more sense for the company to continue to buy back stock as well.

Let’s assume Cellnex retires a net amount of 15M shares per year. That would result in a net share count of 655M shares by the end of 2027. Applying the midpoint of the anticipated RLFCF in 2027 of 2.1B EUR to a reduced share count would result in a RLFCF per share of 3.20 EUR. And this indicates the stock is trading at just over 10 times the recurring levered free cash flow (after taking sustaining capex into account).

We know its US counterparts (AMT and CCI) are currently trading at a multiple of 17.6 and 18.3 times their respective FFOs in FY 2027. Keep in mind the FFO result of both large tower companies does not include the maintenance capex, while the Cellnex RLFCF does take this into account. Applying the same multiple of approximately 18 times the FFO onto Cellnex’ RLFCF would indicate a fair value of 55 EUR based on a 685M share count and 57.6 EUR based on a 655M net share count by the end of 2027.

The risks associated with an investment in Cellnex Telecom

Cellnex has its tenants under long-term contracts, and those contracts include clauses that protect Cellnex against inflationary issues. As tenants like consistency, the churn tends to be very low as Cellnex is the largest provider of phone towers in Europe and the company enjoys an excellent reputation.

I think the main risk associated with an investment in Cellnex is an interest rate risk. As the company has approximately 18B EUR in gross financial debt (excluding lease liabilities), and a 100 bps increase in the average cost of debt would have a negative impact of almost 200M EUR per year, and close to 150M EUR per year on an after-tax basis. This could reduce the earnings by approximately 20–25 cents per share. Why this wouldn’t be a major headwind, it would definitely be a headwind.

Fortunately, most of the company’s debt was issued in Euro and the ECB has been reducing the benchmark interest rate for the past 12 months. The interest rates on the financial markets and bond markets are decreasing at a slower pace, but I don’t expect any major issues when the company has to refinance 430M EUR of debt this year and 1.7B EUR of debt in 2026.

Additionally, as the company’s RLFCF and reported FCF after growth initiatives continues to increase, the market

Investment thesis

Cellnex Telecom hasn’t paid a meaningful dividend in its history, but that’s changing now. While the dividend this year is rather symbolic (just a few cents per share, like the previous years), Cellnex has pledged to spend at least 500M EUR per year on cash dividends from next year on. This still doesn’t make the company a high-yield stock, but it may attract a different type of investor. Let’s also not forget the minimum dividend represents a payout ratio of just around 25% of the levered free cash flow result, so Cellnex has a lot of discretion to "throttle" the dividends (and share buybacks).

That’s why I have a long position in Cellnex and why I continue to write put options on market weakness. The stock is currently trading at just 12 times this year’s expected RLFCF, and this is expected to decrease to less than 11 based on the projections for 2027.

Its North American peers are trading at a substantially higher valuation and even if I would apply a 15% lower multiple and assign a multiple of 15 times the anticipated RLFCF, I end up with a fair value of 45-48 EUR by 2027. Using the midpoint of that target (46.5 EUR), the potential annualized return is approximately 18% per year for the next two years. Adding in the dividend would push the total return to in excess of 20% per year.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chevron: Buy In Before The Rebound
data.description:
Chevron Corp. (NYSE:CVX) has not been able yet to rebound from the sharp valuation haircut the oil company suffered in April, leading to a 5% dividend yield that is now available to passive income investors.

Though crude oil prices also recently slumped and corrected down to $64/barrel, Chevron Corp. has high profitability and exposure to growth in a number of projects ranging from West Africa to the Gulf of America.

The oil company’s stock is selling for a mere 14.2 times 2026 estimated earnings, and Chevron Corp. is anticipated to grow its profits by 25% next year. The lack of an upside retracement in the last two months suggests unrealized upside potential for the stock of Chevron Corp. and the risk/reward relationship continues to be tilted upward, in my view.

In my last piece on Chevron Corp., I pointed to the upside that was inherent in the oil company’s upstream segment, which is the company expanded its production.

Chevron Corp.’s stock price has not recovered yet from April’s market convulsions, and I think this might be a good time to back up the truck with Chevron Corp.’s stock.

Production Growth, Upstream Profit Contraction

There are two conflicting factors at work within Chevron Corp.’s portfolio:

  1. Crude oil prices have continued to fall in 2025. While falling prices have weighed on upstream profitability, Chevron Corp. is compensating for a softer price backdrop by raising its production.
  2. In Q1'25, Chevron Corp. had a net production of 3,353 MboeD which the company is planning to grow 6-8% this year, primarily because of growth in the Permian, the Gulf of Mexico, and Kazakhstan.

The 5-quarter trend for Chevron Corp.’s upstream profits is negative, thanks to falling prices for crude oil. Presently, crude oil of West Texas Intermediate grade is selling for $63.60/barrel, but prices fell below the $60/barrel price market in April and May amid fears that new tariffs were dragging the world economy into a potential recession.

Crude oil prices have rebounded again lately, however, which could point to profit tailwinds for Chevron Corp. In the second quarter, if crude oil price increases prove to be of a lasting nature.

In the first quarter, Chevron Corp. produced $3.8 billion in profits from its upstream activities, which reflected a 28% decrease over the comparable period last year. Chevron Corp.’s Q1'25 downstream profits were $325 million and also decreased, by a total of 58%, thanks to lower margins on refined product sales.

That being said, Chevron Corp. maintained robust profitability and the company is starting up a couple of new projects that should yield production gains for the oil company moving forward. Among the new project startups are new operations in the Gulf of America (Whale and Ballymore), Australia (Wheatstone), and West Africa (Nigeria and Angola).

Though the trend in upstream profits is negative in the 5-quarter window shown below, Chevron Corp.’s profit prospects could brighten up in the event that U.S.-Sino relations improve and new trade uncertainty is avoided.

The U.S. economy is also profiting from gradually receding inflation (which decreased to just 2.3% in the month of April) and the U.S. economy is anticipated to grow in 2025, all of which could help a recovery in crude oil prices.

In addition, as mentioned, Chevron Corp. Is planning to see a high-single-digit increase in production in 2025, which could help the company counteract lower crude oil prices in its upstream segment.

Chevron Corp: Lack Of Upside Retracement Signifies Investment Opportunity

Shifting trade policy in the United States and the announcement of new tariffs on Liberation Day (April 2, 2025) have resulted in a substantial valuation correction for Chevron Corp as well as other oil companies in the U.S.

Chevron Corp.’s price has shown no signs of a recovery so far, but a bottom formation is clearly visible in the energy company’s stock chart, suggesting that the stock could be gathering strength for a breakout.

Chevron Corp. is selling for a 2026e profit multiple of 14.2 times and the oil company is expected to produce 25% profit growth next year, undoubtedly driven by the oil companies' multiple upstream projects around the world.

Chevron Corp.’s growth rate is the highest in the oil peer group as the market models only 20% growth for ExxonMobil Corp. (XOM) and half that, 10%, for ConocoPhillips (COP).

Though Chevron Corp. has the highest profit multiple, the stock offers passive income investors the most generous dividend yield of 5%, suggesting that the oil company might indeed be undervalued.

ExxonMobil Corp. is selling for 13.1 times, 2026 profit multiple, whereas ConocoPhillips changes hands for 12.1x times next year’s estimated earnings. Both companies have substantially lower yield offers for their shareholders.

In my view, Chevron Corp.’s high 5% yield could potentially establish a floor underneath the oil company’s present stock price and limit additional downside from here.

Moreover, Chevron Corp. is growing its dividend, which suggests that passive income investors a rising yield for their invested capital in the future.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Disappoint

A crude oil price correction to the downside could lead Chevron Corp. into a profit recession and put downward pressure on the oil company’s cash flow.

So far, Chevron Corp.’s earnings setup is still very decent as the company earned billions in profits in Q1'25, and with new project startups poised to add new production, Chevron Corp. might even see earnings and cash flow growth moving forward. That being said, periods of falling crude prices are poised to correlate with substantial valuation headwinds for the energy sector.

In the event of a drastic decrease in crude oil prices in 2025, Chevron Corp. is poised to see a severe multiple contraction.

My Takeaway

Chevron Corp. exhibits robust profitability in its upstream business, despite growing pressure on the company’s profits. Even with a decrease in crude oil prices, however, Chevron Corp. should be able to remain highly profitable and be able to afford a higher dividend for investors in 2025.

In my view, Chevron Corp. should be able to recover from the valuation haircut in the energy industry as trade uncertainty resolves and inflation continues to recede.

Though it is true that the energy company has seen falling upstream and downstream profits in the last year, catalyzed by lower crude oil prices, Chevron Corp. is still very much profitable and should be able to counteract at least some headwinds to earnings in a lower-price environment by growing its production.

Chevron Corp.’s lack of a valuation rebound is making the stock quite irresistible, as far as I am concerned, as the oil company is now paying investors a juicy 5% yield and investors potentially can ride a valuation rebound here as well. Buy.

data.title: QQA: New Option Strategy ETF In The Nasdaq 100
data.description:
QQA strategy

The Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF (NASDAQ:QQA) is an actively managed fund in the derivative income category launched on 07/17/2024. QQA has an attractive 12-month distribution yield of 8.63% and an expense ratio of 0.29% (waived from inception to 6/30/2025). Distributions are paid monthly.

As described by Invesco, the fund provides “exposure to the Nasdaq-100 Index combined with an active option income overlay for income generation”.

The fund invests in Nasdaq 100 stocks and up to 10% of its assets in equity-linked notes (ELNs) embedding options-based income strategies involving the Nasdaq 100 Index or ETFs tracking it. The fund may also hold a substantial part of assets in cash equivalents for risk management purposes.

ELNs are hybrid assets including equity securities and related options in a note (unsecured senior debt of the issuer). They typically embed covered calls and cash-secured puts strategies, meaning QQA delegates income generation to ELN issuers. It keeps the expense ratio low, although management costs of options strategies are passed in the performance of ELNs. Additionally, the fund may also directly invest in options and futures. This article will use as a benchmark the Nasdaq 100 Index, represented in the next chart by Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ).

Portfolio

As of writing, QQA has 76% of asset value in the Nasdaq 100 constituents with weights reflecting the index, about 19% in a money market fund, and about 5% in ELNs.

The top 10 companies, listed in the next table, represent 39.9% of assets and the three largest positions (Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Apple (AAPL)) are between 5.7% and 6.6%.

The Nasdaq 100, whose sector breakdown is reported below based on QQQ, is overweight in information technology (57.2% of asset value) and has significant exposure in consumer discretionary (19.7%). Other sectors are below 6%.

Fundamentals

Performance

Obviously, a track record of less than one year is too short to assess the long-term potential of the fund. In this time frame, QQA is close behind QQQ in total return (the gap is 1.45 percentage points).

However, QQA price return since inception is close to zero:

Distribution history is also too short to judge ELN strategies. It is quite stable for now.

QQA vs. competitors

QQA is the smallest of these funds in assets under management. It has the cheapest expense ratio (on par with GPIQ), and the second-lowest yield. It comes in fourth position for total return and risk-adjusted return (measured by the Sharpe ratio in the table above) since July 2024. QQQI and QQQH have the best Sharpe ratios, on par, on this point. QQQI has a higher yield and return, while QQQH is more compelling for risk-averse investors, based on volatility and drawdown.

Takeaway

The Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF has an 8.63% yield from option strategies in the Nasdaq 100. QQA has 3/4 of assets in Nasdaq 100 stocks and delegates income generation to equity-linked notes. QQA is almost on par with QQQ in total return since July 2024. However, in this short track record, QQQI and QQQH look more compelling than QQA, the former for its higher yield and return, the latter for its lower volatility and shallower drawdown.

data.title: The Jobs Report Did Not Indicate Strength
data.description:
Stocks surged on Friday with news that the US economy created 139,000 jobs in May, which was modestly higher than the consensus estimate of 126,000. The unemployment rate held steady at 4.2%, while wages increased 0.4% for the month and 3.9% over the past year. That is an uptick from the 3.8% rate the month before. Evidently, this report shelved concerns about a downturn in economic activity, due to tariffs and the ongoing trade war. As a result, investors drove the S&P 500 index above 6,000 for the first time since February, and bond yields soared with the 10-year Treasury yield back above 4.5%, while the 30-year yield neared 5%.

If Friday’s stock market surge was based on a strong jobs report for May, it was a false premise. The jobs report did not indicate strength. Even if it did, employment is a lagging indicator that tells us very little about what lies ahead. Investors would be better served to focus on leading indicators but first let me explain why this report was not as strong as reported.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics estimates how many jobs are created each month with a margin of error that ranges +/- 100,000 jobs. As more data is collected, the number is revised. The direction of the revisions gives you a sense of the underlying trend. Little mention was made of the fact that the number of jobs created in the prior two months were revised down by 95,000. That means the net job gain for May was 44,000. This is more in line with the 37,000 private jobs that the ADP payroll report indicates were created. It is estimated that the economy needs to create at least 100,000 jobs per month to absorb new entrants into the workforce. Based on recent data, we continue to accomplish that goal, but more timely data raises doubts.

While weekly unemployment claims are still within the range they have held the past two years, claims have been in an uptrend since the beginning of the year. We need to exceed 260,000 to raise serious concerns.

The uptrend in claims is likely to continue based on job cut announcements reported by Challenger, Grey & Christmas, which have risen significantly this year. In May, there were 22,492 job cuts announced in the service sector, which is the highest total for one month in May 2020. The service sector is our engine of growth. Furthermore, employers have cut 696,309 jobs so far this year, which is an 80% increase from the year-to-date number one year ago. This is a significantly negative rate of change. According to Andrew Challenger, “tariffs, funding cuts, consumer spending, and overall economic pessimism are putting intense pressure on companies’ workforces.”

This doesn’t signify economic strength, nor does the business optimism survey from the Association of International Certified Professional Accountants. In May, only 27% of executives were confident about the economic outlook over the coming year, which is down from 47% during the first quarter and 67% in the fourth quarter of last year. This is leading to delayed hiring and expansion plans.

Therefore, if we want to try to explain why stock prices surged on Friday, I think the jobs report for May from the BLS is a very poor excuse. Instead, I think it is nothing more than pure momentum chasing momentum, which runs counter to deteriorating fundamentals. That said, the labor market is still holding up, and wage growth of 3.9% indicated that workers are still realizing real (inflation-adjusted) wage growth. This should help to sustain the economic expansion. That said, the jobs report is not a lagging indicator.

Lost on most economists is the fact that consumer spending is the primary driver of job creation and not the other way around. Granted, more jobs lead to more income, which then leads to more consumer spending, but new jobs are a function if increased demand for goods and services. Those jobs are not created out of thin air. Therefore, investors should be focusing on consumer spending growth, which is the tip of the spear in terms of leading economic indicators.

Spending has been resilient through April, but the strength was likely bolstered by consumers who were trying to front-run price increases from tariffs in the same way that businesses ramped imports to build pre-tariff inventories. That is likely to result in a downturn in spending growth this summer, which is aggravated by price increases from tariffs. Then we will see an increase in job losses and unemployment claims. Finally, the BLS jobs numbers will reflect these developments at summer’s end or in the fall.

data.title: GameStop, Oracle Headline Earnings Next Week As Investors Eye Trade Talks, Inflation Data, WWDC25
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's focus next week will be on further trade developments and key inflation data. U.S. and Chinese officials including U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and Trade Representative Ambassador Jamieson Greer are set to meet in London on Monday. On Wednesday, the consumer price index report for May will be published, followed by the producer price index report on Thursday. Also grabbing the spotlight will be Apple's (AAPL) WWDC 2025 event, kicking off on Monday. The event is expected to feature major announcements and a first look at new updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.

Video game retailer GameStop (GME), cloud software firm Oracle (ORCL) and Photoshop creator Adobe (ADBE) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, June 9: Casey's General Stores (CASY), Calavo Growers (CVGW), Lakeland Industries (LAKE). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, June 10: GameStop, J.M. Smucker (SJM), United Natural Foods (UNFI), GitLab (GTLB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, June 11: Oracle, Chewy (CHWY), SailPoint (SAIL). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, June 12: Adobe, Lovesac (LOVE). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Applied Digital (APLD) and Ondas Holdings (ONDS) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include SafeSpace Global (OTCPK:SSGC), M3-Brigade Acquisition V. (MBAV), and Regulus Therapeutics (RGLS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.A), Abacus Global Management (ABL) and Arrive AI (ARAI).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Steven Madden (SHOO), Ross Stores (ROST), Nvidia (NVDA), Domino's Pizza (DPZ), and Gilead Sciences (GILD). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO watch: San Francisco-based fintech company Chime Financial (CHYM) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. At the top end of the expected range, Chime could raise up to $832 million, with a fully diluted valuation of about $11 billion. Voyager Technologies (VOYG) is also set to launch its IPO. The Denver-based aerospace and defense company is targeting a valuation of approximately $1.6 billion. The quiet period ends on eToro Group (ETOR) to free up analysts to post ratings. The IPO lock-up period expires for certain blocks of shares of ServiceTitan (TTAN).

Investor Events: Apple's WWDC 2025 event in Cupertino, California will take the spotlight next week, scheduled from June 9 to 13. The event is expected to feature major announcements and a first look at new updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS. The Databricks Data + AI Summit will run from June 9 to 12, featuring talks from top executives from Databricks, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, and Microsoft (MSFT) CEO Satya Nadella.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Airbnb: Investing For Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Airbnb’s (NASDAQ:ABNB) core growth may have slowed in the recent results, but this alternative accommodation platform has plenty more in store for guests, hosts, and investors. Airbnb's engine has been its main earner since its founding in 2008, but more recently the company has been investing in its business on three fronts to ensure growth remains for the long term.

  1. Airbnb has been improving its core service, listening to feedback, and making many incremental additions and changes to make the service even better. These include better pricing visibility, the option for co-hosting, Guest Favourites, and actively removing lower quality listings, all of which and more add together to make the experience better for all users.

  2. Airbnb has been investing in increasing its penetration in geographies that have good tourism industries, but where Airbnb is underrepresented.

  3. Airbnb has also been working on a revamped Experiences offering, which aims to massively expand the total addressable market for Airbnb, and give users more reasons to open the Airbnb app than just accommodation, ultimately increasing the share of the wallet Airbnb can earn per user.

The investments ABNB has made into all of the above have suppressed margins in recent periods, but this is now a sunk cost, with continued development likely not as onerous, there will be additional budget freed up for sales and marketing expenditure to boost user growth.

In this article, I’ll discuss each of these, as well as Airbnb’s recent earnings in the context of their historical financials. I’ll then discuss how I think about the growth outlook in the context of my valuation. I initiate coverage of ABNB with a buy.

Q1’25 earnings, growth slowed

ABNB reported continued deceleration of their top-line growth of 6%, or 8% in constant currency, to $2.3 billion. Free cash flow came in at $1.8 billion and my preferred measure, trailing twelve-month FCF, was steady at $4.4 billion for a 39% margin to revenue.

While this level of growth may seem concerning for some who are used to Airbnb reporting consistent double-digit earnings growth, it is a little distorted because of the calendar. Firstly, Easter fell in Q1 last year and Q2 this year. This made Q1’24 a difficult period to lap because it was an artificially strong period for bookings. It also means that what was lost in Q1’25 will be made up for in Q2’25, and this was clarified on the call as being worth about 2 percentage points. In constant currency, if Easter was in the same period, growth could have been 10%. Q1’24 also had the benefit of an additional day due to February's Leap Day, which also made the comparison period a little stronger.

This chart shows the seasonality of Airbnb’s business model over the course of a year but the trajectory is undoubtedly going in the right direction.

To smooth this out, looking at the trailing twelve-month figures each quarter is helpful. Notice as well how steady the take rate is. Take rate is not something I would like to see Airbnb leveraging for growth, as the model works because the economics are shared with the host, and if this balance is upset (either by taking more from the host or charging the guest more as a service fee, thereby also harming the economics to the host), if they were to get this wrong, it could have negative consequences so I am happy to see this remain consistent in the post-pandemic years.

Growth in nights & experiences booked and gross booking value (GBV) were broadly in line with revenue growth, meaning price appreciation was not a major factor in the revenue growth.

Interestingly, Net Income fell 42% to $154 million. Normally, with a steady company like Airbnb, I would expect someone off to be the culprit, and to a small extent they were (some write-downs of subsidiaries), but it was also due to hiring, additional stock-based compensation (SBC), and lower interest income.

Lower interest income is the most interesting here, as Airbnb has been over-earning because of it in the last couple of years as interest rates have increased. An attractive quirk of ABNB’s business model is that the guest pays for their stay up front, but the host is not paid until after the stay. Airbnb holds this cash in the meantime and earns the going rate of interest on it. It is akin to the “float” of an insurance company: the cash doesn’t belong to them, but they are able to earn a return on it while they have it. As Airbnb has grown, this has grown into a substantial amount to the point that it is now a substantial bulk of their net income, and therefore any material change in the rate of interest earned will have a material impact on the net income of the company.

The following chart shows how Airbnb’s unearned revenue has moved over time and I have also overlaid the interest income.

Airbnb Today

Airbnb is now solidly and predictably profitable. Post-covid and post-IPO in 2020, there was a genuine inflection point where the company tidied up its expense structure and turned on the cash tap. This is clearly seen in the following Adjusted EBITDA and margin charts but also in the free cash flow. The following two charts show Adjusted EBITDA (which excludes SBC). The first one is on a quarterly basis and the second is on a trailing twelve-month basis.

Growth in TTM Adjusted EBITDA has slowed over the past 6 quarters despite growing revenue, and this appears to be due to the investment made into the product, as management has been talking about building a new tech stack that involved adding hundreds of new features and also can support new services and experiences. This was released on May 13.

This theme is also reflected in the EBITDA margins, which have declined slightly over the past 4 quarters. It is not so obvious on the quarterly EBITDA margin chart, however is clear on a TTM basis.

Investing for Long-Term Growth

The result should be viewed from the lens of a company that is investing in its own business for long-term growth. It will certainly be a slow grind, pressure on margins will remain while they invest in onboarding Experience hosts and entice users to try out the services, but the intention from Airbnb is clear that there could be multiple billion dollar businesses in what they’ve launched. Services include personalized professional cooking experiences, massages, photography, spa treatments, personal training, and hair and beauty appointments: all things that one might typically find at a hotel, but have been reimagined for the localized Airbnb experience.

Airbnb is also investing in international expansion outside of its core US, Canada, Australia, UK, and France markets. The focus for expansion is on Brazil, Mexico, Germany, Spain, Italy, China, India, Korea, and Japan. These markets are growing twice that of the core markets and are a good indicator of the response to the localization of the product, the localization of the marketing, and the enhanced features that have been rolling out. The expansion of markets and the addition of Services and Experiences are key reasons why Chesky thinks Airbnb can return to double-digit growth.

Plenty of Dry Powder

Airbnb is a serious cash machine. Of the $1.8 billion in free cash the company brought in, it spent $807 million buying back shares and has $11.5 billion remaining in the bank (note: this does not include the $9.2 billion it holds on behalf of guests/hosts). Airbnb is becoming a serious stock cannibal as it continues to hoover up its own shares. Despite the buyback, diluted shares increased slightly on a q/q basis due to performance stock issuance in the quarter. This seems to be normal for March, and I expect the trend to continue downward over time and indeed accelerate as ABNB is very capital-light and doesn’t have a need for such a high balance of cash to fund its growth ambitions. We could even see a dividend introduced in the future.

Gross Booking Value (GBV) is clearly growing, which is a good sign, as is the growth in unearned revenue (discussed above). Both these point to revenue growth in future periods. The take rate has also steadied, and I think this is a level we can expect it to remain, as it is a good balance between extracting the economics from the transaction from Airbnb’s point of view and sharing the economics with hosts while also providing value to guests. This is all shown in the below chart.

Outlook

ABNB expects revenue to grow 9%-11% in Q2’25 to between $2.99 billion to $3.05 billion. Due to the significant nature of the Summer Release, accelerated marketing expense is expected to impact margins for the quarter. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter is expected to be flat to slightly down for the quarter, which was 32.5% in Q2’24.

ABNB reiterated their 2025 EBITDA margin target of 34.5%, which is slightly lower than the Adjusted EBITDA margin achieved in 2024, primarily due to the investment involved in re-launching Experiences and Services.

On the conference call, CEO Brian Chesky noted the environment seems uncertain but emphasized Airbnb’s ability to adapt, just like they did when starting the business in the GFC and through Covid.

Airbnb relaunches Experiences, Adds Services

How should investors think about these additions to the core accommodation service, especially considering the first launch did not amount to much?

When Airbnb first launched Experiences pre-pandemic, it did not succeed because inventory and available locations were both limited and never really received the focus and push from the company to give it a good go. The pandemic also killed what momentum the feature did have, and management decided it would be better to start from scratch than persist with the integration in its current state.

Airbnb then embarked on a large project to rebuild the platform infrastructure for a more seamless experience between booking accommodation and experience. From my own in-app research, the available offers seem very refined and in his Summer Release 2025 address, Chesky confirmed that each host has been vetted and are highly experienced in their craft.

The first thing I noticed, however, was that the services clearly sit at the premium to luxury end of the spectrum. Prices start at around $50 per person for basic offerings but tend to have 3 tiers. This is somewhat in contrast to the image Airbnb initially projected with accommodation, which was not just a unique place to stay but tended to be a cheaper alternative than a hotel. This quashes this notion.

That is not necessarily a negative. When one travels, budgets tend to go out the window, and travelers tend to be more inclined to splurge for a unique experience. Chesky is angling this launch as an attempt to match the in-house services you can get at a hotel, and I think that is a sound approach. But for me, the Services go so much further. Sure, at a hotel you can go to the restaurant downstairs, but can you have a Michelin Bib chef come and cook you a personalized meal for you and your fellow travelers? Or will the hotel send a personal photographer around the city with you?

Photography is actually a great example of this dynamic I am explaining. On a particularly special trip, I could absolutely see myself spending a few hundred dollars to get some once in a lifetime professional photos in the city I am visiting. I may only do it once on a month-long trip, but that still gives Airbnb more share of the wallet of a trip than it had previously, which is exactly their goal.

And that, I think, is the point. Airbnb has spent a considerable amount of capital building Services and Experiences, but now that this significant sunk cost is in the past, the cost to maintain will be not much different from maintaining the existing platform. The number of guests using services is likely to be a small percentage, but it opens up opportunities for special experiences and events. Need some family portraits taken in your home city? Move over Google, just flick through Airbnb. Hosting a bachelor party and want something a bit different from going out for dinner and drinks? Hire a personal chef.

I don’t think the services launched will have anyone booking things weekly (which is where Chesky wants to move towards), but for special occasions, it gives users a great option to easily find an expert in the city in which they live or the city in which they are visiting.

What will the financial impact be? I don’t know, but I do think it will be a slow uptake. CFO Ellie Mertz has said they don’t anticipate much impact in 2025, but Chesky has also said there could be some lines that reach a billion dollars in scale in the future. As for when or if this is achieved, your guess is as good as mine. I expect the financial impact to be modest in the next few years, with the potential for stronger contributions as users become more comfortable with the offerings.

Modeling the Impact of Services and Experiences

I have been modeling Airbnb for a few years now, but this is the first time I have shared it on Seeking Alpha. For 2026 and 2027, I have added half a percentage point of revenue growth from the addition of Services and Experiences to my pre-existing growth assumptions. In the next 3 years, I have assumed 1 percentage point of additional growth, and 2 percentage points for the following 4 years. This implies that in 2034, Experiences and Services could add over $500 million in revenue, which is still a pretty small contribution. You can see the additional revenue and growth contribution in the below chart.

Chesky is expecting the impact to be much larger than this. For a nascent business, this could be wildly overstated, or it could be wildly understated. However, thinking through the inputs enables us to understand what is feasible and what is not. And seeing the impact on valuation gives us an understanding of whether the business might be undervalued or not relative to our expectations.

Including these assumptions into my 10-year discounted cash flow model means a 10.5% revenue CAGR and with some slight margin expansion, a 12.5% EBIT CAGR. Using a weighted average cost of capital of 8.7% and a terminal growth rate of 3%, we can estimate a valuation of Airbnb of about $146. Importantly, I have only included interest earned on customer cash, as I add back the Airbnb-owned cash to the equity value. Including both in the cash flows would double count Airbnb’s cash position.

We can now do some scenario planning based on the potential impact of Services and Experiences. If we assume zero revenue contribution, Revenue CAGR drops to 9.3% per annum and valuation drops to $135. However, if, as Chesky expects, the contribution from Services and Experiences is much more material than I have assumed, let’s say by doubling what I initially included, the value of the company could be $159.

The various growth assumptions of each scenario are shown in the chart below.

This provides a range of outcomes for Airbnb, but the bottom line is that Airbnb remains attractively priced at current levels regardless of the outcome. By my modeling, Airbnb is fairly valued if no revenue contribution at all comes from Services and Experiences (which would seem unlikely), and is undervalued if Services and Experiences have more than a modest contribution over the next decade.

Risks

Given this discussion, the risks may seem somewhat obvious; this thesis is predicated on a successful launch of Services and Experiences, which are for all intents and purposes new businesses because they will involve a change of behavior. So if the uptake in the medium term isn’t material, then Airbnb will have wasted a lot of capital expenditure on building the product (less important because that’s in the past) but will also waste a lot of operational expenditure on marketing to encourage usage (far more important because it is in the future).

Similarly, Airbnb is spending a lot on geographical expansion, as discussed above. They are using a playbook that has been successful in the past, so I back the company to be successful, but it is far from guaranteed.

One risk that will hang over the company for some time is regulatory risk. Cities like New York and New Orleans have famously been antagonists to the holiday rental platform and most recently in February 2025, Airbnb has gone as far as to sue the City of New Orleans for an overly punitive enforcement regime. Airbnb indeed has a good relationship with many cities across the world, but if the tide turns en masse it would be negative to the business model of Airbnb.

Conclusion

Don’t be too concerned with the seemingly low growth provided in Q1, a company doesn’t always have to grow top line at above 20% for it to be a good investment. Growth was lower than usual because of seasonal factors and currency. The former will reverse, and the latter is completely out of their control. Airbnb is currently in an investment phase which is modestly compressing margins, but the growth on offer, even if it is lower than previous years, is still sufficient to make the valuation look attractive.

data.title: Meta: Reality Labs Could Take Off Sooner Than Later
data.description:
After calling the bottom of the selloff and the breakout that led to the current rally, I am now revisiting my bull case on Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) as there have been a few developments worth integrating into my thesis.

From a price action perspective, the chart below looks like a textbook V-shape recovery.

As you are about to find out, the main reason I'm reaffirming my strong buy rating is due to several positive developments in the Reality Labs segment. I admit that previously I didn't particularly like this business unit due to its abysmal operating loss compared to minimal revenue contribution. However, I believe the upcoming ramp up in Meta's physical stores, along with the partnership with Anduril, will drive some attention in this segment.

That said, my bull case does come with risks, and I’ve noticed some growing tensions in Europe, which I’ll discuss in this article.

A Note On The Reality Labs Segment

Reality Labs, the segment that encompasses the Quest VR headsets, the Ray-Ban smart glasses, and the Horizon Worlds platform, accounts for a very small part of Meta's revenue compared to the family of apps segment.

To be precise, it accounted for only 1.2% of total revenue in the past 12 months. So, why am I even bothering with this segment? (some may say)

Well, it turns out that the segment is highly unprofitable.

As seen above, the EBIT of the Reality Labs segment was negative $18 billion for the past 12 months. Considering that over the same period, the revenue of this segment was only $2.1 billion, the cash burn in this segment is abysmal, to say the least.

Before I dive into my point, a quick clarification, as I've seen some confusion with this segment in the comments section. The Llama business is not part of the Reality Labs segment. Nor are the costs related to operating the data centers to support this LLM, the AI ads generation, or Meta's ML algorithms to display content or ads on its family of apps. Reality Labs is mainly the hardware business (i.e. the VRs and smart glasses) and the remaining part of the Metaverse business (i.e. the Horizon Worlds).

The main driver behind the operating loss in this segment is employee compensation. Below is a breakdown.

Now, even though Reality Labs accounts for 1% of total revenue [TTM], the employee compensation in this segment accounts for a whopping 24.7% of the total in 2024.

To me, that's not encouraging at all! That's why any positive development in this segment (i.e. anything can bring in more revenue) is music to my ears, which brings me to the next point.

Meta's Strategy For VR Headsets

Fun fact: did you know that Meta has just one physical store, located in Burlingame, California?

Neither did I until I came across this article. To be honest, considering the $18 billion operating loss [TTM] in the RL segment, I assumed they were operating hundreds of stores, just like Apple does. Wrong!

It seems that Meta is (finally!) ramping up their physical presence across the US. Unfortunately, there are no concrete details on their expansion strategy. Nonetheless, this is great news for people to experience their technology. You never know if you need something until you see it, you try it, and you convince yourself that you needed it.

Quick side note. Here is how I got my Quest 2s. I needed a big TV. I didn't want to buy it (I only buy expensive call options, not expensive real stuff). I never tried a VR headset and I was very skeptical. I went to an Apple Store to try the Apple Vision Pro experience. I was sold on the VR technology the very first minute of using it. I went online and bought the Quest 2s from a second hand store. I'll be direct. I would have happily paid the full price for a Quest 2s if it was available at that Apple store, just for the convenience of having it on the spot.

The same goes for the Ray-Ban smart glasses, which only sold about 1 million units in 2024. This tech is so futuristic that only a few people have actually tried it. I believe most consumers are as skeptical as I used to be. But once they get the chance to try it, many might change their minds, just like I did.

Aside from the consumer VR headsets, Meta secured last week a partnership with Anduril. The partnership focuses on XR headsets designed to help soldiers see better and control autonomous systems during missions.

Why is this announcement so exciting?

Well, it turns out that the US Army has a $22 billion project to develop wearable combat technology. Meta and Anduril have already submitted their bid for a $100 million contract with the Pentagon. That said, unlike most startups that rely on the contract to develop the technology, both Meta and Anduril have already funded the development with private capital. This is key. To me, this is a sign that both companies are confident that they will win this, and other military contracts.

Yet another big announcement came in just a few days ago when it was reported that Meta has held talks with Disney A24 to get exclusive content rights for its new VR headset. We are talking about immersive content where the users feel like they are part of the movie. This, combined with the physical store strategy makes complete sense to me, as it gives Meta some exciting content to showcase in their stores to skeptics of the VR technology.

Finally, even though I don't expect to see the Reality Labs segment profitable in the next twelve months, mainly due to operating expenses related to the new stores, I do believe we will see an increase in revenue driven by consumer and military VR headsets. As to the Ray-Ban smart glasses, I remain skeptical until I get my hands on a pair and give them a try.

Risks

Once again, I reiterate the regulatory risks from my previous thesis, and I add a few ones.

Last week, a report came in stating that Germany is weighing on a 10% tax for Meta and Google. That's not exactly good news, especially since Meta's revenue from Europe [TTM] made up 23% of its total sales. Since these tax plans could quickly spread to other left-leaning countries in Europe, it's a major risk worth keeping in mind.

Other risks include the delay of the Behemoth LLM. According to some reports, this model was delayed to June, from its expected release in April, prior to the LlamaCon event. Is this overly concerning? Probably not, considering that Meta has (finally!) released its Llama API. This is great news for developers (like myself, in my third part-time job) who can now integrate the Llama 4 LLM into their code. I've been spending quite a lot of my (gambling) cash on OpenAI's API, so having access to Llama's API could make me (and other developers) give it a try. That said, I don't expect any meaningful improvements in Meta's "other segment" revenue this quarter, as the API is only in its free limited preview.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I reiterate my strong buy rating, with a price target in the mid $700s by the next few months.

There are a number of favorable developments since my latest coverage outshadows the negative ones. To be specific, the physical store expansion, the potential deal with Hollywood, and the partnership with Anduril could improve the revenue of this segment in the near to mid-term. That said, I don't expect profitability in this segment any time soon. Furthermore, I believe the Llama API will be a key revenue driver within the "other" segment once the free beta version ends.

On the risk side, I still think Europe (particularly the left-leaning countries) is a big risk from a regulatory and tax perspective. If the trade tensions between the US and Europe intensify (in the unlikely case they don't make a trade deal), I believe more countries will follow Germany's 10% tax initiative on internet services (i.e. ads).

data.title: NewMed Energy Q1: Buy Rating As Revenue Grew
data.description:
Since initiating coverage in NewMed Energy (OTCPK:DKDRF) last March 2024, the stock unit returned nearly 63%, outpacing the S&P 500's 14.3% return. By November of that year, shares continued to trend upward, returning 35.2% while the S&P 500 increased by 2.23%. In May, the company filed its first-quarter results and 2024 annual report.

What should shareholders do with their partnership holding after a review of the results?

About NewMed Energy

NewMed Energy is an Eastern Mediterranean natural gas firm. It is a key partner in the exploration, development, production, and sale of natural gas and condensate. It has agreements with firms in Egypt, Jordan, and the UAE. Before I initiated coverage on DKDRF units, around two years ago, shares likely traded at a discount in response to the Middle East conflict.

First Quarter 2025 Results

In the first quarter, NewMed reported an increase in natural gas and condensate sales. Net revenue rose to $245.6 million, up from $226.1 million from last year. This increased mainly from a rise in natural gas sold from the company's Leviathan reservoir. It rose from ~2.60 BCM last year to ~2.95 BCM in Q1.

Condensate sales of ~237,000 barrels increased from ~52,000 barrels last year, contributing to the revenue increase. A decrease in the average price per thermal unit (MMBTU) from $6.16 per MMBTU to $5.85 per MMBTU offset the results. Here are the gas quantities sold, broken down by geographic location:

Expenses fell slightly as the cost of production declined. After expenses from depreciation, depletion, amortization, and other direct expenses, total expenses were still lower Y/Y.

Net profit fell to $116.4 million as financial expenses increased slightly. Financial income fell from around $6.2 million, compared to $27.4 million last year. The company experienced a drop mainly from the revaluation of the royalties receivable. This resulted in approximately $22.0 million reported last year.

Recent Developments

NewMed planned to drill an exploration well in the Vinekh prospect. The project budget is around $54 million. It also funded an exploration well, which is either in the Vinekh prospect or around the Block. If successful, it would lead to an increase in proven reserves.

The Board approved an additional $29 million for the Leviathan project. The total approved budget is $131.3 million, whereas its partnership share is $59.5 million. The operator of the Leviathan project is Chevron (CVX) Mediterranean Ltd.

Strong Financial Health

On May 11, NewMed redeemed the second series of bonds early, compared to the original maturity date of June 30. The early redemption amounting to $400 million out of $600 million took place on May 29.

The company has a provision of credit facilities with two Israeli banks. As shown below, NewMed meets the covenants. In particular, it has around $500 million in liquidity, compared to the minimum of $20 million.

The total financial debt of approximately $1.6 billion does not exceed the $3 billion limitation. At 4.3, the firm meets the "ratio between surplus sources and the sum of the credit facilities with every bank separately shall be no less than 1."

At the end of the quarter, NewMed held cash and cash equivalents of $99.7 million.

Opportunity for Income Investors

Net cash flow growth generated by the partnership reaffirms the attractiveness of this investment. The company used net cash flow prudently for investment activities in the last quarter. It used the cash flow to distribute profits and to buy back the Leviathan bonds. Last year, NewMed used the cash flow to repay a loan from a banking corporation. That is in addition to buying back Leviathan bonds as well.

Leviathan bonds have a BB rating from Fitch with a stable outlook.

The distribution is another attractive reason to hold DKDRF stock. On March 9, the board of directors approved a profit distribution of $0.05112 per participation unit, totaling $60 million. The board approved the same amount on May 11, distributing the profits on June 5.

Risks

NewMed has market risks. The value of its future production-based royalties might change by up to 2%. In a sensitivity analysis, the company applied a profit change of +/- 2.0%. Royalties from the Karish and Tanin leases vary by between -$19.3 million and $22.8 million:

Changes in natural gas prices are macroeconomic risks that investors should consider. Valued at $262.1 million as of March 31, 2025, this may vary from -$44.5 million on a 30% loss from changes in natural gas prices. Royalties increase to another $36.8 million in profits if natural gas prices rise.

Your Takeaway

NewMed is an undercovered investment that does not get enough attention. The regular distribution is a reason to hold this company. It complements the cash flow growth, where the income might rise as energy sales increase.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Piper Sandler: Liberation Day Should Lift Brokerage In Q2
data.description:
Piper Sandler (NYSE:PIPR) logged a decent quarter, but some of the caveats we pointed out last coverage are now explaining the results. Volatility was good for the brokerage business in Q1, and Q2 has more volatility in store, though equity brokerage may be a more sustained beneficiary of a fat-tailed environment. Other than that, volatility, and particularly changes in the yield curve, are not going to help the business much. ECM may see some improvements after things have calmed down as we come towards the end of Q2, but we're less convinced about DCM, which doesn't like yield curve volatility. Mid-market is still the right place to be in advisory, with Trump not being the catalyst that the industry expected for large ticket deals. With Evercore (EVR) down more than PIPR though more exposed to sensitive high-ticket activity, PIPR continues to be the preferred relative pick. We like PIPR into Q2 as they do benefit from volatility in brokerage, but it's hard to tell if they'll close fewer deals in advisory thanks to Liberation Day in Q2. Characteristics of the mid-market may indicate that it's not going to be too affected.

Results Breakdown

As usual, advisory shops give colourful and useful commentary on the business, which is highly linked to the macro/financial environment. They are still talking about the return of sponsors.

With the roughly 340 companies we have sold to financial sponsors that still reside in their portfolios, position us well as the incumbent bank to capitalize on round trip sales, continuation vehicles, and financing opportunities from our private equity client base.

Chad Abraham, PIPR CEO

While he is right that at some point activity will come back, PEs aren't going to dispose of their businesses and make new investments with costs of capital high (poorer selling multiples, harder to generate leveraged returns). There hasn't been a cut yet, and we question if one is coming that soon. While inflation expectations are relatively contained, they are up and the trade negotiations are ongoing, therefore their impacts are still unclear on imported inflation effects. As we've been saying for years, PE is going to wait. Though, the 340 commentary and round trip sale figure is one to remember since it sort of gives an idea of possible backlog if they get repeat business. 5% commission on 340 $500 mn mid-market transactions could mean upwards of $8 billion in business for PIPR over some undetermined period of time.

Above is our favourite chart provided by the company. Our comments:

  • As we said in the last coverage, ECM and DCM which go into corporate financing have low visibility. As soon as the markets turned for the worse in Q1, this business declined. Sequential declines were already being observed when we covered them at the beginning of the year, also meaning that the activity spurt was a latent business that was waiting for the opportunity to move forward, and got quickly exhausted. Predicting into Q2, DCM will suffer in line with muni discussed below (we assume), while ECM will have a mixed quarter since volatility in April would push back activities though things are looking momentarily better now in areas like IPO. However, it could be quite bad as well if PIPR isn't in the key deals.
  • Muni financing was up, but the fact that the yield curve is shifting upwards but also penalising long-horizon bonds since the quarter's end means that municipal governments may stay on the sidelines in the coming quarters and prefer not to come to market to fund new infrastructure. Management indicated that the difficulty in pricing transactions was a problem in April. It is normal for debt raising to stop when the yield curve is in flux because getting into a debt arrangement and then being proven wrong by the yield curve is a difficult thing to explain to stakeholders—similar to DCM particularly given PIPR's strong franchise with depositors who have to be very careful after the SVB debacle.
  • In a similar vein, volatility is usually good for brokerage. Fixed income was up in this quarter. This was mainly on better real yields but also general yield curve speculation. As for what might come next, we discuss some of the yield curve dynamics here, particularly around the USD reserve currency status as a speculative factor, considering the rise in long-term rates. Maybe next quarter will still be good though due to Liberation Day and associated volatility in bond markets once again. Primary market activity is not good, but the secondary market could be alright given the major changes in the yield curve in just the last month. But once allocators are positioned, they may stay put, with actions already taken to be safe in fixed income a fatter tail environment.
  • Equity brokerage also grew on the heightened volatility in Q1 around the tech sell-off. The quarter in March ended. We may see a really solid coming quarter on account of Liberation Day. It could be all alright going beyond Q2 as well, given the fat-tailed environment. Equity brokerage tends to be more consistent and predictably pro-volatility than fixed-income brokerage.

Bottom Line

The big one is advisory services. It did great. Mid-market continues to be the place to be in Q1. We question whether major consolidations will occur after all, and we think the considerable policy changes may prevent deals, even with the easier FTC situation compared to under Biden. The mid-market is less sensitive to these structural dynamics. Evercore was of similar value to PIPR according to our crude methodology in our last article, but they are more exposed to large-ticket activity. With them both trading down to similar degrees, we have more reason now to prefer PIPR. We think Q2 will be alright in brokerage, bad in ECM and DCM and muni financing. Advisory is a tougher call. We guess that the idiosyncratic nature of mid-market might save PIPR's Q2, but there probably was a lot of apprehension around deals due to Liberation Day. Mid-market deals in the US may be less concerned with tariff effects, as there are plenty of regional businesses in the US that aren't concerned by global supply chains. The US has plenty of its economy not exposed to imports. Though it's possible transactions got paused for a while and Q2 might be victim to timing effects, or worse to actually cancelled deals. We can't really say we have conviction at this moment given the surer things elsewhere in our coverage universe.

data.title: I'm Still Bullish On Tempus AI
data.description:
Tempus AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:TEM) is a technology company that enables precision medicine. Its core business is genomics and data services, but recently, it has expanded its vision into AI for healthcare applications. While this has resulted in impressive growth, a recent Seeking Alpha update noted a short report on TEM. But, on balance, I still believe the evidence largely favors the bull case for TEM at this point.

Healthcare AI Facing Tough Questions

Tempus AI is a healthcare technology company that enables precision diagnostic and treatment decisions based on artificial intelligence [AI]. Their AI is trained with large-scale genomic and clinical data. And back in February 2025, I wrote a “Buy on dips” thesis for TEM due to their innovative AI-driven healthcare platform and their promising acquisition of Ambry Genetics. But also because I see great potential in the various emerging applications of their AI capabilities in healthcare, like the Tempus One agent and xH whole-genome sequencing test. Plus, since my initial analysis, there have been important updates presented in the Q1 2025 report that are also worth discussing.

You see, broadly speaking, TEM sequences tumor and germline DNA and RNA, and aggregates de-identified patient records. They collect this data from thousands of hospitals and train machine-learning models to generate useful data for oncologists and researchers. Their core products are: 1) Genomic testing services, with NGS panels for several diseases like solid tumors and hematologic malignancies that feed high-quality data into TEM’s AI platform.

Moreover, TEM’s 2) Data and analytics include a proprietary database of over 40 million patient records, licensed to pharmaceutical and biotech companies. Good examples of these are deals with AstraZeneca (AZN) and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) for drug discovery, biomarker validation, and real-world evidence generation. And lastly, TEM has 3) AI-driven applications like Tempus One agent, a generative AI tool, and an LLM trained on clinical and molecular data. Another app is “Olivia” for patient use, which extracts insights from unstructured medical records, recommends personalized treatment alternatives, and streamlines payer submissions.

Now, more recently, TEM reported a solid Q1 2025. They reached $255.7 million in total revenues, with a 75.4% increase from $145.8 million in Q1 2024. This growth was driven principally by Ambry’s hereditary testing business, which contributed $63.5 million in only two months of operations. Also, their legacy oncology testing Genomics revenue reached $193.8 million, up 88.9% YoY, and Data and Services revenue totaled $61.9 million, up 43.2%. So, TEM is, for all intents and purposes, a rapidly growing healthcare AI company (at least on paper).

Path to AI Scale In Healthcare

It’s also worth mentioning that TEM’s acquisition of Ambry (completed in February 2025) gave them proprietary hereditary-testing data for their AI platform. Ambry’s Q1 revenues were also impressive ($63.5 million), but integration costs led to a net cash outlay of $375 million. So, its full benefits should be more visible in their Q2 2025 report.

Interestingly, TEM continued its acquisition spree with Deep 6 AI (March 2025), which added patient-matching technology to its app portfolio. Deep 6 is a platform that uses AI to match patients to clinical trials and generate real-world evidence [RWE] by mining data from structured and unstructured digital medical records. This platform was already integrated with over 750 healthcare sites and covered over 30 million patient records. And this M&A spree shows they intend to rapidly grow their data, which is broadly aligned with their healthcare AI ambitions.

Looking ahead, TEM expects to launch xT CDx and xH whole-genome sequencing as well. xT CDx is an FDA-approved tissue NGS test, with a national rollout, priced at $4,500 per test. Basically, xT CDx runs on Illumina NovaSeq 6000 sequencers to train genomic algorithms for molecular testing. xH is a whole-genome sequencing for research use only [RUO] for hematologic malignancies. Commercial availability of this product is anticipated in 2026. But probably their biggest win was their agreement with AZN and Pathos AI to develop a multimodal foundation model in oncology to discover drug targets and build therapeutics.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, TEM trades at a $11.0 billion market cap, so it’s still a sizable operation despite the recent pullback. It’s roughly down 31.2% since the last time I covered it, and at the time, I thought buying it on dips was the best way to go. In my view, that has largely played out, and in the meantime, TEM’s bull case has played out.

We’ve seen continued improvement based largely on its business moat with data. TEM’s data lake and AI tools have indeed helped it secure a $200 million deal with AstraZeneca and Pathos. Similarly, they’ve entered into a long-term collaboration with Boehringer Ingelheim for their oncology pipeline. And probably more importantly, TEM’s latest investor presentation quoted that they now have >4000 sites, >40 million patient records, and roughly 300 petabytes of data. In my view, this shows that their data moat is incredibly valuable for other companies as it is today, but it’s also fertile ground for training AI apps with it.

Moreover, they actually beat my initial revenue target post-acquisition of approximately $1.0 billion per year. Management now projects 2025’s revenues at $1.25 billion, implying an 80% YoY growth, and comfortably exceeding my previous forecast. I also initially expected that margins could turn positive within two to three years, but management now projects positive (adjusted) EBITDA in the second half of 2025. Lastly, I also thought that TEM might tap into the capital markets for more financing if growth didn’t play out, but the opposite happened.

And in this sense, I think the picture is mixed because, on one hand, growth has actually exceeded my expectations. However, their cash has declined, and their Q1 report did show a substantial uptick in cash burn. When I covered them back in February 2025, they held about $466.3 million in liquid resources. And today they have $151.6 million in cash and $67.2 million in marketable securities. That amounts to roughly $218.8 million in liquid resources as of their latest report. Naturally, this drop is largely due to their acquisition of Ambry, so it’s not as alarming.

However, if we exclude the acquisition, I still estimate their quarterly cash burn jumped to $107.7 million in Q1 2025 from $47.3 million in Q4 2024. Note that I got these figures by simply adding TEM’s cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And at the latest cash burn rate of $107.7 million, their implied runway would be just 2.0 quarters, which is alarming. Still, one can argue that Ambry will now start helping with their margins and revenue growth. Plus, if TEM’s cash flow turns positive in 2H2025 (in EBITDA terms) as management expects, then this runway estimate might be too bearish.

Nevertheless, I have to concede that just looking at the numbers, this is a crucial risk for TEM right now. If their cash burn doesn’t decline significantly in Q2 2025 (next earnings report in August 2025), then TEM could be in trouble without additional financing. In my view, this makes the upcoming earnings report extremely important.

Additionally, I want to quickly bring up some relevant points worth considering regarding the ongoing controversy on TEM. I won't rehash those claims here. You can read the reports for yourself (1, 2). Instead, I'll simply stick to the fundamentals that, I believe, are relevant.

In my view, as long as we continue to see the growth thesis play out, and cash burn declines and trends into positive cash flow, then TEM should do fine in the long run. I also believe TEM's $200 million deal with AstraZeneca is worth contextualizing a bit further. This deal was explicitly for “data licensing and model development fees.” This is not part of TEM’s explicit AI Applications revenue, but I argue that it’s clearly tightly linked to TEM’s broader strategic vision in AI and healthcare. I bring this up because if you consider that caveat, TEM doesn't seem like AI Hype.

Still, one of the main issues I do see with TEM at this juncture is the pending decision regarding MolDx and TEM's xM MRD (Minimal Residual Disease cancer tests). Essentially, we still need to wait and see if Medicare gives TEM the thumbs-up for reimbursement on their MRD assays. Naturally, this is a typical risk (and binary) in healthcare and biotech companies. But, if they can’t reimburse these MRD assays, then TEM’s growth rate could stall as it becomes less competitive, and it could become an issue given the cash burn concerns I’ve outlined.

Also, as far as I can tell, TEM’s MRD assays are relatively similar in nature to Guardant Health’s (GH) Reveal oncology tests, which are already approved for Medicare. And TEM has roughly 40 million patient records to support the argument for reimbursing its MRD assays and their usefulness. Naturally, I anticipate this process will probably take several months until it’s definitely approved for reimbursement. Management itself said this approval process is "playing out," so overall I'd say the odds favor TEM for now on this front as well.

Lastly, I think it's vital to remember that TEM's latest 10-Q “Legal Matters” section clearly states there are “no material” legal matters as of Q1 2025. And in their 2024 10-K report, Deloitte’s opinion was that TEM’s financials are, “in all material respects,” in conformity with US GAAP. So, for now, I believe we can also trust TEM’s financials.

Moreover, valuation-wise, TEM is now starting to look compelling again. Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on TEM currently projects they’ll generate roughly $1.6 billion in revenues by next year. That implies a forward P/S of 6.9, which is definitely higher than its peers’ forward P/S of just 3.3. However, TEM’s impressive forward revenue growth rate of 43.0% vastly outpaces its peers’ growth of just 8.0%. And, because of that, I believe its valuation premium seems largely fair, if not undervalued. My main concerns at this point are their cash burn and the potential "uncertainty" overhang on the share price.

Conclusion: Reiterated “Buy”

Overall, TEM’s bull case has mostly strengthened since my initial analysis of them. But, at the end of the day, I think fear can definitely weigh on an otherwise promising growth story. And since TEM’s valuation is largely dependent on its growth potential, the ongoing drama could cap the upside prospects for the stock price to some extent. But all in all, I consider it fair to restate my initial “Buy” rating on dips for TEM. Since we’ve seen a dip already, it’s probably a good time to start slowly accumulating the shares at these levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
